description:
You are a database reliability engineer who wants to get a quick feel for how long a typical backup + restore workflow takes on this host.

    1.  The directory that must be benchmarked already exists:  

            /home/user/sample_db_data

        It contains exactly three CSV files.  Do **not** rename, remove, or modify these files.
            • customers.csv – 50 lines  
            • orders.csv    – 100 lines  
            • products.csv  – 30 lines  

    2.  Benchmark requirements  
        a. Compress the entire directory into a gzip-compressed tarball.  
        b. Immediately restore (untar) that archive into a separate directory.  
        c. Measure **only** the wall-clock time (in seconds, with millisecond precision, e.g. 0.123) of each of the two steps.  
           Any standard Linux timing tool is acceptable (for example, “/usr/bin/time -f "%e" …”).  
        d. Create a dedicated results directory if it does not yet exist:

            /home/user/backup_benchmark

    3.  Required artefacts  
        • The compressed archive created during the test must be saved as  

              /home/user/backup_benchmark/sample_db_backup.tar.gz  

        • Extraction of that archive must end up in  

              /home/user/backup_benchmark/restore_test  

        • A single log file **must** be produced at  

              /home/user/backup_benchmark/backup_performance.log  

          The log must contain exactly two **non-empty** lines, **no header line**, each recording one benchmarked step in strict CSV format:

              ISO_8601_UTC_TIMESTAMP,operation,duration_seconds

          where  
              – ISO_8601_UTC_TIMESTAMP is in the form YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ssZ (note the trailing “Z”).  
              – operation is the literal string “compress_sample_db” for the first line and “restore_sample_db” for the second line.  
              – duration_seconds is a decimal number with exactly three digits after the decimal point (e.g. 1.237).

          Example (illustrative, your times will differ):

              2023-10-06T14:21:05Z,compress_sample_db,0.842  
              2023-10-06T14:21:06Z,restore_sample_db,1.103

    4.  Validation hints  
        • The automated grader will fail the task if there are anything other than two CSV lines, if the operations are misspelled, if the timestamp is not in UTC (“Z”), or if the duration does not have millisecond precision.  
        • It will also verify that:
            ─ /home/user/backup_benchmark/sample_db_backup.tar.gz exists and is a valid gzip file.  
            ─ /home/user/backup_benchmark/restore_test exists and contains exactly the same three CSV files as /home/user/sample_db_data.  
        • You do **not** need sudo/root privileges.  
        • Do not remove the original /home/user/sample_db_data directory.

    Your goal: produce the archive, perform the restore test, measure both durations, and create the correctly-formatted backup_performance.log file in one terminal session.
truth:
Initial conditions created by the test harness
    ----------------------------------------------
    1. Directory /home/user/sample_db_data already exists with three files:

       /home/user/sample_db_data/customers.csv
          - 50 lines, first line: "id,name"
       /home/user/sample_db_data/orders.csv
          - 100 lines, first line: "id,customer_id,total"
       /home/user/sample_db_data/products.csv
          - 30 lines, first line: "id,name,price"

       (The exact remaining contents of each CSV are not important for grading; only the line counts and file names matter.)

    Grading expectations after the agent finishes
    ---------------------------------------------
    A.  /home/user/backup_benchmark/backup_performance.log must exist.
        1. It must contain exactly two lines, line-feed terminated, no blank lines.
        2. Line 1 must match the regex:
              ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z,compress_sample_db,[0-9]+\.[0-9]{3}$
        3. Line 2 must match the regex:
              ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z,restore_sample_db,[0-9]+\.[0-9]{3}$

    B.  /home/user/backup_benchmark/sample_db_backup.tar.gz
        1. File exists.
        2. “file” command identifies it as “gzip compressed data”.

    C.  /home/user/backup_benchmark/restore_test
        1. Directory exists.
        2. Contains exactly three files named customers.csv, orders.csv, products.csv.
        3. Each extracted file’s SHA256 checksum matches the corresponding file inside /home/user/sample_db_data.

    D.  No extra files other than those expressly asked for may appear inside /home/user/backup_benchmark.

    The grader will perform checksum comparisons, regex matching and file existence checks to validate the above conditions.
